description: Full styling design pass per DEC-024. Originally deferred four times (DEC-024 тЖТ DEC-026 тЖТ DEC-037 тЖТ DEC-042) on the CBM-redo-friction trigger principle. **Reopened as a parallel workstream by DEC-076 (05-16-26)** because the trigger condition (CBM-redo Phase 1 surfacing visual friction on the four v0.4 methodology panels) cannot fire in v0.5's timeframe тАФ CBM redo waits on v0.5 (DEC-075) plus the paper-test (DEC-077) plus Phase 1 itself, and the four-deferral pattern with progressively-elaborate trigger conditions that never fire has become an anti-pattern. The pass establishes a coherent visual language for the v2 desktop application: design tokens (palette with semantic naming, typography scale, spacing scale, radius/border/elevation tokens, density target), visual decisions for major component classes (panels, sidebar, buttons, form controls, dialogs, tables), and implementation across all governance and methodology panels. **Workstream structure (per `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-workstream-plan.md`):** two conversations тАФ Conversation 1 produces the design pass document at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-design-pass.md`; Conversation 2 produces the styling PRD, implementation plan, and slice build prompts. Strawman five build slices follow (foundation; governance panel retrofit; methodology panel retrofit; dialog and form-control polish; closeout). **Boundary discipline with v0.5 (per DEC-076):** styling owns the visual layer (QSS, design tokens, panel chrome, sidebar visuals, About dialog, hover/focus/disabled states); v0.5 owns the data/routing layer (config, ActiveEngagementContext, alembic, API routing, engagement entity). One coupling point: v0.5's engagement panel inherits whatever tokens are current at slice land time; either order ships fine. **Version-bundling question** (v0.5 vs. v0.6) deferred to the styling workstream's Conversation 2.
title: Full styling design pass per DEC-024

description: DEC-043 resolved the SES-010 identifier-asymmetry friction via option (B) тАФ ``GET /<entity>/next-identifier`` helpers for all twelve prefixed entity types. Option (C) тАФ making ``identifier`` optional in POST bodies with server-side auto-assignment on omission тАФ is the cleaner end state but was deferred from v0.4 because it changes existing endpoint contracts (gray-area under the kickoff's 'doesn't alter v0.3's storage shape' rule) and deserves its own design pass. Open design questions: (1) how to disambiguate client-omitted identifier (auto-assign) from client-specified-empty-string identifier (validation error)? (2) does this apply to versioned entity types like charter and status, whose identifiers are version numbers? (3) does the MCP server need any change beyond what the API change implies? Target release: v0.5 or later, depending on scope competition.
title: Make `identifier` optional in POST bodies (SES-010 option C)

description: DEC-039 deferred persona from v0.4's minimum-viable inventory because evolved Phase 1's interview guide v0.2 explicitly excludes persona elicitation in Phase 1 тАФ persona context comes from pre-engagement reading of operational role definitions, used as consultant background rather than captured as records. Phase 2 or 3 of the evolved methodology may surface persona records (actors performing processes, role-based access boundaries). The persona entity type design should follow the workstream pattern (one Claude.ai conversation produces the schema spec, design-only, separate from build). Target release: v0.5+, conditional on CBM redo Phase 2 or 3 surfacing need.
title: Persona entity type for v0.5+ (deferred from v0.4 minimum-viable inventory)

description: DEC-039 deferred fields, requirements, manual-config items, and test specs from v0.4's minimum-viable inventory. These are Phase 3+ entity types in the evolved methodology (Iteration Build and Deploy through Engagement Closure and Adoption). Each will need its own schema-design conversation following the workstream pattern established in v0.4. Field is the most urgent of the four because entity (shipped thin in v0.4) gains real utility only when fields can attach. Likely v0.5 ships field; later releases ship the rest. Target release: v0.5+ for field; v0.6+ for the rest, conditional on CBM redo progression and v2 patterns established in earlier releases.
title: Additional methodology entity types for v0.5+: field, requirement, manual_config, test_spec

description: DEC-039 ships process in v0.4 with a thin Phase-1-only schema: identifier, name, brief description, priority classification, domain reference, process-to-process connections. Phase 3 of the evolved methodology (Iteration Build and Deploy) produces full Process Documents with steps, actors (personas), entity-field touches, triggers, outcomes, edge cases. Growing process's schema to host this content is v0.5+ work that depends on field (PI-004) and persona (PI-003) existing as records. Likely a migration story rather than re-design (thin schema designed with growth in mind), but the migration scope is non-trivial. Target release: v0.5+ after persona and field land, conditional on CBM redo reaching Phase 3.
title: Process schema growth beyond Phase 1 thin shape (full process definition)

description: DEC-046 establishes the parent-prefix field-naming convention for methodology entities forward-only; governance entities (DEC, SES, RSK, PI, TOP, REF, CHR, STA) retain bare field names (status, title, name, description, etc.) until this retrofit lands. Retrofit scope: Alembic migrations renaming columns on eight existing tables; access-layer method updates across crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/; REST API serialization updates; MCP tool input/output updates; UI dialog updates (every QLineEdit, QComboBox, and table model column bound to a renamed field); DB-export JSON snapshot regeneration with rewriting of committed snapshots in git history; backward-compat considerations for any external scripts hitting the REST API or MCP. Substantial migration. The v0.4-build-planning conversation decides whether to pull this retrofit into v0.4 scope (significant additional work) or defer to v0.5+. The methodology workstream's specs ship with the new convention regardless of when this retrofit lands.
title: Retrofit governance entities to parent-prefix field-naming convention

description: The existing CBM methodology uses 2-letter domain codes (MN for Mentoring, MR for Mentor Recruitment, CR for Client Recruiting, FU for Fundraising) as prefixes for process and entity identifiers (MN-INTAKE, MR-RECRUIT, etc.). The Phase 1 evolved-methodology interview guide section 7.2 says the Domain Inventory includes 'name in client's language and a structural code' but does not specify whether downstream process/entity codes continue to use mnemonic prefixes in the evolved methodology. DEC-045 defers domain_short_code from the v0.4 minimum-viable shape pending CBM-redo signal. If real-engagement Phase 3 work confirms that mnemonic prefixes still pull weight for human-readable process/entity identifier construction, a v0.5 schema migration adds domain_short_code (str, 2-4 uppercase letters, unique within engagement); the process and entity schemas adopt it for human-readable identifier prefixes alongside their numeric identifiers. If the evolved methodology converges on sequential identifiers without mnemonic prefixes, this PI closes as not-needed.
title: domain.short_code field for mnemonic references and downstream identifier prefixes

description: Surfaced by SES-012 close-out friction. The current pattern requires Doug to either (a) copy-paste session content into the v0.3 desktop New Session dialog and run a per-conversation apply script for decisions/PIs/references, or (b) run a generic apply_close_out.py script that processes a JSON payload file (mid-SES-012 mitigation). Both still require a manual command after each schema-design conversation. The architectural fix: build an inbox folder watcher into the v0.3 desktop application. The app monitors a known directory (e.g., crmbuilder-v2/inbox/ or PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/), detects new JSON payload files, prompts the user to review (showing a summary of what would be written), and on confirmation imports all records via the existing access layer. Processed files move to inbox/processed/ for traceability. Idempotent on re-run (HTTP 409 = already present, skip). Implementation scope: file-watch infrastructure (already exists for db-export refresh тАФ possibly reusable), payload-schema validation, summary-and-confirm modal, error reporting per record. The v0.4-build-planning conversation decides whether to pull this into v0.4 scope (eliminates close-out friction for all four workstream conversations) or defer to v0.5+. If deferred, the apply_close_out.py script remains the close-out pattern.
title: Inbox folder watcher in v0.3 desktop app for close-out JSON payloads

description: DEC-054 defers a Domains column on the master pane of the Entities panel to v0.5+, jointly enabled by PI-007 (domain.short_code field). The column renders affiliated domains as comma-separated short codes (e.g., 'MN, MR, FU') once short codes exist; without them, DOM-NNN identifiers don't tell a consultant anything at a glance and the column's UX value is lost. Implementation scope when both PI-007 and PI-009 land together: (a) access-layer batched-join query (list_entities_with_affiliations() or equivalent) returning each entity's affiliated-domain short codes alongside its scalar fields without N+1 queries; (b) ListDetailPanel view-model update to surface the new column; (c) column-width and sorting behavior decisions (sort by what? probably alphabetical-by-short-code-list with secondary sort by entity_name). Prioritization signal: CBM redo will reveal whether the absence of the column in v0.4 is high-friction (do consultants frequently click into the detail pane to check affiliations? do they request a list view grouped by domain?). If high-friction, PI-009 + PI-007 move up in v0.5 priority; if low-friction, they stay queued.
title: Master-pane Domains column on the Entities panel (paired with PI-007 short codes)

description: DEC-051 and DEC-053 jointly defer two entity-schema growth areas to v0.5+: (a) entity variants тАФ the Mentor Contact / Client Contact pattern from CBM history where two related-but-distinct entities share a base type but carry different fields and role semantics; (b) entity base-type / kind classification тАФ person / organization / event / transaction-style categorization that informs CRM-engine selection and field-shape defaults. Both are intentionally absent from v0.4: variants because Phase 1 doesn't produce them and v0.4 has no fields to vary across them; base-type/kind because it's Phase 3 territory tied to CRM-engine specifics that v0.4's thin shape stays neutral about. Tracked separately from PI-004 (which groups field/requirement/manual_config/test_spec as new methodology entity types) because PI-010 is entity-schema growth тАФ extending the existing entity type тАФ not introducing new entity types. Variant mechanism TBD: candidates include self-referential FK (entity_parent_identifier), references-entity edge (entity_variant_of_entity), or a separate variant entity type sitting between base entity and field. Mechanism choice is a v0.5+ design conversation. Workaround in v0.4: variants are distinguished by suffixing entity_name ('Contact тАФ Mentor', 'Contact тАФ Client'); CBM redo will reveal how much friction the workaround creates and inform mechanism design. Base-type/kind classification: candidate v0.5+ shape is an enum field (entity_kind: person | organization | event | transaction | other) on the entity table, with validation that informs Phase 3 field-shape defaults and Phase 5 CRM-evaluation scoring.
title: Entity-schema v0.5+ extensions тАФ variants and base-type/kind classification

description: DEC-057 adopted process_classification (mission_critical / supporting / deferred / unclassified) as the methodology priority classification per Principle 3. During the conversation the user observed that this is a classification, not a conventional scalar priority, and the methodology's three buckets don't answer the implementation-ranking question тАФ 'of the mission-critical processes, which do we build first?'. A separate scalar implementation-priority field (working name process_priority, values Critical / High / Medium / Low or 1-5 or similar) is deferred to v0.5+. The deferral reasoning: Phase 1 does not formally produce per-process implementation priority тАФ that information emerges from Phase 2 Slice Planning, which v0.4 schema does not yet host as content. When Phase 2 (Slice Planning) iteration content lands in v0.5+, the scalar priority field is part of that work, scoped per-iteration rather than global to the process record (the same process might have different scalar priorities in different iterations). Implementation when this PI lands: (a) add process_priority field with chosen value space; (b) decide whether the field is global on the process record or per-iteration via a join table to a future Iteration entity; (c) UI: master-pane column showing classification + priority paired (e.g., 'mission_critical / High'); (d) priority-transition validation if any (probably free movement per Principle 7). Prioritization signal: CBM redo Phase 1 will reveal whether consultants want implementation-priority ranking at Phase 1 cadence (probably no тАФ Phase 1 outputs classification, not implementation order). Real signal comes from Phase 2 when slice planning content needs to host iteration-membership decisions.
title: Future scalar implementation-priority field on process for ranking within classification buckets

description:
DEC-061 adopted Option A (thinnest) for the crm_candidate field inventory under minimum-viable v0.4 scope. Four optional metadata fields named in the schema-design-kickoff-crm_candidate.md document were deferred from v0.4 to v0.5+ under this planning item:

- crm_candidate_vendor_url тАФ optional free-text URL field pointing to the vendor's product or documentation page; no enum constraint, light URL-shape validation only.
- crm_candidate_hosting_type тАФ optional enum: 'cloud' | 'self_hosted' | 'both'. Captures the Phase 1 hosting selection criterion as a structured value.
- crm_candidate_license_type тАФ optional enum: 'open_source' | 'commercial' | 'freemium'. Captures the Phase 1 license/cost-model selection criterion as a structured value.
- crm_candidate_price_tier тАФ optional enum: 'free' | 'paid'. Coarse capture of the Phase 1 budget selection criterion.

The deferral rationale is the minimum-viable v0.4 scope principle plus the small per-engagement candidate count (2-3 records typically), which makes scannability gains from structured fields small relative to their schema-and-UI-and-migration cost. The Phase 1 selection criteria all live in crm_candidate_fit_reason prose in v0.4.

Triggers to pull this PI to v0.5 ahead of other v0.5 candidates:
- CBM-redo reveals that consultants miss scannable classification at the master-pane level when reviewing the candidate set across iterations (the CBM-redo-friction trigger pattern established by DEC-042 for PI-001).
- Phase 3 deployment-log entity type or Phase 4 Comparison Artifact entity type (both deferred to v0.5+ entity types under PI-004 expansion) need to relate to structured candidate metadata for cross-iteration analysis.
- Multi-engagement analytics emerge as a v2 use case where parsing crm_candidate_fit_reason prose for hosting / license / budget across engagements is infeasible.

Implementation scope when pulled:
- Alembic migration extending the crm_candidates table with four optional new columns: vendor_url TEXT (nullable, no CHECK constraint), hosting_type TEXT (nullable, CHECK constraint on the three-value enum), license_type TEXT (nullable, CHECK constraint on the three-value enum), price_tier TEXT (nullable, CHECK constraint on the two-value enum). All new columns are nullable; existing crm_candidate records persist with the new columns NULL.
- Access-layer validators for the three enum fields (the URL field gets light client-side and access-layer URL-shape validation; the enums match the standard pattern used by domain_status, entity_status, process_classification).
- vocab.py expansion: not needed; the enums are non-reference values stored directly in the entity table.
- UI work: combo boxes for the three enums in the create/edit dialog and detail pane; free-text URL editor for vendor_url; possibly a master-pane column extension to render one or more of the new classification fields as scannable badges. Specific UI shape decided at v0.5+ planning time.
- Acceptance criteria additions for the four new fields: format validation, NULL-on-existing-records, transition validation if any enum values are constrained to transition rules (working assumption: no тАФ classification fields here describe stable product attributes, not lifecycle states).

No backward-incompatible changes. The crm_candidate API surface stays at the standard 8 endpoints; the new fields are accepted as optional in POST / PATCH / PUT bodies and surfaced in GET responses.
title: crm_candidate structured-metadata enums (vendor_url, hosting_type, license_type, price_tier) for v0.5+

description:
Target version: v0.5+.

The original methodology named Cross-Domain Service (Notes, Email, Calendar, Surveys) as a methodology entity type consumed across domains. The evolved methodology has not yet resolved whether Cross-Domain Service remains a distinct entity type, is subsumed into `process` once `process_kind` lands in v0.5+, or is dropped. The v0.4 schema workstream did not address the question and the original methodology-entity-schema-planning-prompt did not flag the gap. Resolution waits on CBM-redo signal: if Phase 1 surfaces work that the evolved methodology treats as cross-domain in nature, evaluate whether `process` with a future `process_kind` field is the right home, or whether a separate `cross_domain_service` entity type is warranted. Decision gate: at the first CBM-redo Phase 1 conversation that touches Notes / Email / Calendar / Surveys content. Surfaced by the 05-14-26 reconciliation conversation (SES-018).
title: Cross-Domain Service representation

description:
Target version: v0.5+.

The catalog ingestion PRD's section 3.3 sketched a hybrid integration pattern: methodology entities carry an optional `primary_catalog_entity_id` FK plus DEC-006 universal references for weak ties. The four v0.4 methodology schemas (`domain`, `entity`, `process`, `crm_candidate`) do not implement the FK because their MVS thin shape doesn't carry the field-level entity definitions that would need it. When PI-004's `field` entity type lands in v0.5+, the catalog FK question must be settled: (a) integer FK to `catalog_entity.id` vs text FK to `catalog_entity.catalog_id` per the catalog DEC-065 stable-identifier convention; (b) FK on `entity` vs on `field`; (c) how universal-references weak ties interact with the FK semantics. Scope: tied to PI-004 prerequisite; design conversation opens when PI-004's design conversation does, not independently. Surfaced by the 05-14-26 reconciliation conversation (SES-018).
title: Catalog FK integration for methodology entities

description:
Target version: v0.5+ or later.

DEC-008 prescribes that v2 produces renders, not authored copies тАФ Word documents, deployment YAML, and JSON exports are all generated from the database, not maintained alongside it. v0.4 ships the storage and UI surface for the four MVS methodology entity types but zero renderer work. Three render targets are forecast: (a) .docx generation for Domain Inventory and Phase 1 Prioritized Backbone documents (per evolved-methodology Phase 1 outputs); (b) YAML generation for the v1 deployment engine (current YAML lives in client repositories as hand-authored files); (c) per-entity JSON exports for git-diff via the existing DEC-008 hook (these may already exist mechanically through the storage layer's standard export pattern тАФ verify before scoping). Likely a sizable workstream on its own; opening it before v0.4 ships is premature. A separate planning conversation should follow either v0.4 closeout or first CBM-redo iteration, whichever comes first. Surfaced by the 05-14-26 reconciliation conversation (SES-018).
title: Methodology entity renderers

description:
Target version: v0.5+.

The live `/references` router does not enforce per-pair `RELATIONSHIP_RULES` because doing so would break v0.3 regression tests that use cross-pair references like `session тЖТ decision / decided_in`. Per-pair vocab compliance is currently enforced only at the cascading `ReferenceCreateDialog`, which means the desktop UI is safe but the REST surface is not. If the API is consumed only by the desktop UI (which routes through the dialog), the gap is theoretical. If MCP tools or any external script POSTs directly to `/references` with arbitrary kinds, a non-vocab `kind` could land in the database. Resolution: tighten router-level validation while preserving v0.3 cross-pair governance reference behavior тАФ likely by enforcing per-pair `RELATIONSHIP_RULES` only for pairs where rules exist, leaving pairs with no rules (current v0.3 pattern) unconstrained. Surfaced by SES-021 (v0.4 slice C entities panel build) when implementing criterion 14's "POST /references with (entity, domain) and an unsupported kind returns 422" test, which was satisfied at the schema-rejection level rather than at the router-vocab level.
title: Router-level per-pair vocabulary enforcement on `/references`

description:
Anchored by DEC-081. v0.5 ships one API and MCP subprocess per engagement (Option A); multi-tenant single-process server (Option B) is the target architecture for v2's production phase. The migration lands when v2 transitions from prototype (v0.x releases) to production (v1.0). Trigger is a planned transition, not a friction signal (avoiding the PI-001 anti-pattern of friction triggers that never fire).

Scope of the migration when it lands:

1. Refactor every REST endpoint to accept engagement context. Two viable mechanisms: a request header (X-Engagement: ENG-NNN) or a path-prefix (/v1/engagements/ENG-NNN/sessions, etc.). The mechanism is settled when the workstream opens; both options have precedent in similar systems.

2. Refactor the access-layer connection model to hold per-engagement connections. Options include: a connection pool keyed by engagement_identifier opened lazily on first use; eager pool warming at startup; or per-request connection acquire/release with engagement-context binding. The pool strategy is settled by the workstream.

3. Refactor tests for engagement-context fixtures. Every test that today asserts behavior against a single DB needs to express which engagement it operates against. v0.4's test suite is the regression net.

4. Reshape the MCP server to mirror the multi-tenant API. MCP tools either gain an engagement-context input argument or a per-tool engagement selector. Specific shape is settled by the workstream.

5. Remove the kill-relaunch dance from the desktop's engagement-switch path. The desktop's ActiveEngagementContext continues to exist but switching becomes a fast in-process operation rather than a process-lifecycle dance. The 'Switching engagement...' UX indicator becomes vestigial or is removed.

6. Consider adding an activate API endpoint (POST /engagements/{identifier}/activate) at this time. The endpoint becomes possible once the API server can switch engagements without restarting; whether to expose it depends on the workstream's posture on cross-engagement scripting use cases.

Dependencies and ordering: PI-017's workstream is sequenced after v0.5 ships and after enough real-use signal has accumulated (CBM Phase 1 underway or completed; possibly additional client engagements in flight) to validate that the prototype-to-production transition is the right moment. The exact timing is determined at the production-readiness workstream's opening kickoff conversation, not pre-committed here.

Note: this PI is not the same as the deferred 'cross-engagement reporting' or 'engagement-level access control' workstreams. Those are separate v0.6+ candidates. PI-017 is strictly the infrastructure migration; cross-engagement features become technically feasible after PI-017 ships but are out of PI-017's scope.
title: Migrate API and MCP servers to multi-tenant model

description:
**Context.** The CRM Builder YAML schema currently exposes only `oneToMany`, `manyToOne`, and `manyToMany` link types. `oneToOne` is rejected by the validator (`VALID_LINK_TYPES` in `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py:72`). EspoCRM's underlying `EntityManager/action/createLink` API supports oneToOne natively, so this is a translation-layer gap, not a target-system limitation. Two methodology-side artifacts already assume oneToOne is valid (`PRDs/process/interviews/interview-entity-prd.md:338` lists it; `PRDs/_archive/automation-prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-step16-importer-cleanup-3.md:70` accepts it in the Path B importer), so requirements written through the methodology can capture `oneToOne` and then become un-deployable when translated to YAML. The first concrete trigger surfaced 2026-05-19 in the CBM MN-Partner-TEST.yaml / MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml scaffolding (Account 1:1 PartnerProfile, Account 1:1 SponsorProfile) where the workaround is `manyToOne` + an operator-discipline / duplicate-check uniqueness rule.

**Goal.** A YAML author can write `linkType: oneToOne` in a `relationships:` block; the validator accepts it; the engine creates a true 1:1 link in EspoCRM with the correct asymmetric internal types (`hasOne` on one side, `belongsTo` on the inverse).

**Files to touch.**
1. `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py:72` тАФ add `"oneToOne"` to `VALID_LINK_TYPES`.
2. `espo_impl/core/relationship_manager.py` (~line 25, `LINK_TYPE_TO_METADATA`) тАФ current mapping is single-row-per-linkType (`oneToManyтЖТhasMany`, `manyToOneтЖТbelongsTo`, `manyToManyтЖТhasMany`). oneToOne is asymmetric: one side is `hasOne`, the other is `belongsTo`. Restructure to a per-direction mapping, e.g. `(linkType, side) тЖТ metadataType`, or emit oneToOne-aware payload inside `_build_payload`.
3. `espo_impl/core/relationship_manager.py:260` (`_links_match`) тАФ the comparator reads `existing.get("type")` and compares against the expected type from the mapping. With oneToOne it needs to know which side it is reading and which expected type to compare. Probably refactor to compare both sides via two GETs.
4. `espo_impl/core/relationship_manager.py:280` (`_build_payload`) тАФ `linkType` field in the payload is the YAML value verbatim; confirm EspoCRM's `createLink` endpoint accepts the string `"oneToOne"` as-is (it does in current EspoCRM versions). Otherwise emit `hasOne` / `belongsTo` per side.
5. Validator additions in `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py` `_validate_relationship` (~line 2143): oneToOne with `relationName` set should warn (relationName is a manyToMany affordance); oneToOne missing `linkForeign` should be a hard error.

**Steps.**
1. Read the EspoCRM source for `EntityManager.actionCreateLink` to confirm the oneToOne payload shape it expects.
2. Add `oneToOne` to `VALID_LINK_TYPES`; add the asymmetric mapping; refactor `_build_payload` if needed.
3. Refactor `_links_match` to compare oneToOne links correctly (the type read back will differ per side).
4. Add unit tests covering: valid oneToOne accepted; oneToOne without linkForeign rejected; oneToOne with relationName warns.
5. Add an end-to-end test that creates a oneToOne against a real EspoCRM (or a recorded fixture) and verifies the link metadata round-trips.
6. Update `PRDs/product/app-yaml-schema.md` Section 6 (relationships) to document oneToOne.
7. Update `MN-Partner-TEST.yaml` and `MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml` in the CBM repo to use `linkType: oneToOne` once the engine supports it; drop the manyToOne + manual-uniqueness workaround.

**Validation.**
- `uv run pytest tests/ -k relationship` passes.
- Deploy the updated MN-Partner-TEST.yaml against a fresh EspoCRM and confirm: (a) Account.partnerProfile is a single-record link (not a list panel); (b) attempting to link a second PartnerProfile to the same Account is rejected by EspoCRM at the schema level; (c) deleting the linked PartnerProfile clears the link on Account.

**References.**
- Conversation 2026-05-19 (CBM MN partner/sponsor YAML scaffolding).
- Original relationship-manager commit: `f0bf763` (scope explicitly listed three types, no rationale for omitting oneToOne).
- Related engine-bug entries in CLAUDE.md: cross-batch field reference resolution, topological deploy ordering тАФ independent of this PI but in the same engine-evolution backlog.
title: Add oneToOne relationship support to YAML schema

description:
**Context.** The YAML validator's layout-category check is file-scoped. `_validate_layout` in `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py:663-665` builds `field_categories` only from the same YAML's `entity.fields` block:

```python
field_categories = {
    f.category for f in entity.fields if f.category
}
```

This means a layout panel referencing `category: "Sponsor Profile"` only validates if the same YAML file declares a field with that category тАФ it ignores fields declared in sibling YAMLs in the deploy batch and fields already on the server.

This is asymmetric with the same function's handling of field references in `visibleWhen` conditions (line 658-662), which DOES union across `ProgramContext.field_names_for(entity.name)` and `_native_field_names(entity)`. The category check is a one-line addition away from being consistent with the field-name check.

**Triggers seen.** 2026-05-19 conversation on CBM MN-Account / MN-Partner / MN-Sponsor file split. The user wanted to extract Partner-conditional and Sponsor-conditional fields into separate YAMLs while keeping the layout panels in MN-Account.yaml. Failed because the validator wouldn't accept layout panels whose categories were declared in a sibling file. Workaround chosen: keep field+layout in same file or use Account-side reference fields with categories.

**Goal.** A YAML file can declare a layout panel with a category whose backing field is in (a) the same file, (b) a sibling YAML in the same deploy batch, or (c) already on the server. (a) is the current behavior; (b) is a `ProgramContext` consultation; (c) is a server-state consultation when an instance is connected.

**Files to touch.**
1. `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py:663-665` (`_validate_layout`, `field_categories` construction) тАФ union with:
   - Categories from `self._active_context.field_categories_for(entity.name)` (new method on `ProgramContext`, parallels existing `field_names_for`).
   - Categories from server state if an instance is connected (new optional parameter or attribute carrying the instance's metadata).
2. `espo_impl/core/models.py` `ProgramContext` тАФ add `field_categories_by_entity: dict[str, set[str]]` populated from the union of all programs' fields' categories. Parallel to existing `fields_by_entity`.
3. `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py:310` `validate_program_with_context` тАФ already accepts a `ProgramContext`; ensure the new categories field is consumed.
4. (Optional, server-state union) тАФ wire an `EspoAdminClient` reference into the loader so `_native_field_names` and the new categories method can call `GET /Metadata?key=entityDefs.{Entity}.fields` and extract `category` per field. Falls back gracefully to batch-only when no client is available.

**Steps.**
1. Add `field_categories_by_entity` to `ProgramContext`; populate it in the `from_programs` factory.
2. Update `_validate_layout` to union `entity.fields`-derived categories with the context's `field_categories_for(entity.name)`.
3. (Optional in same PI, or split into a sub-PI) Add server-state consultation: when the validator is called with a live client, union in the server's reported categories.
4. Update unit tests in `tests/test_config_loader_layout.py`: a layout panel with a category declared in a sibling YAML passes batch validation; the same panel fails single-file validation (preserving the existing strict mode).
5. Update `PRDs/product/app-yaml-schema.md` to document that categories are resolved across the deploy batch.
6. Add a regression case in the CBM repo's MN test suite: a layout panel referencing a sibling-YAML's category.

**Validation.**
- `uv run pytest tests/ -k layout` passes.
- Deploy the MN-Account / MN-Partner-TEST / MN-Sponsor-TEST batch as currently structured (denormalized reference fields on Account); separately, try a configuration where Sponsor fields live ONLY in MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml and the Sponsor Profile panel is in MN-Account.yaml тАФ the latter currently fails validation; after this PI it should pass.

**References.**
- Conversation 2026-05-19 (CBM partner/sponsor YAML file-split exploration).
- Related engine-bug entry already in CLAUDE.md ("Validator doesn't consult server state for cross-batch field references") тАФ that entry is about field-name resolution; this PI extends the same pattern to categories. Consider grouping the two into a single engine workstream when one is opened.
title: Cross-file category resolution in YAML layout validator

description:
**Context.** EspoCRM stores ONE detail layout per entity. The layout-save endpoint (`PUT {entity}/layout/{layoutType}` in `espo_impl/core/api_client.py:484`) replaces the entire layout payload on each call. The deploy engine processes YAML files one at a time and writes layouts from each file independently. If two or more YAML files target the same entity and both declare a `layout:` block, only the last to deploy survives тАФ the prior file's panels are clobbered.

This breaks the methodology pattern where a domain (e.g., MN, FU, CR) wants to contribute its own panels to a shared native entity (Account, Contact) without one file owning the entire layout. The current workaround is: one file owns the full layout for an entity, others limit themselves to fields and let the layout-owning file reference categories declared elsewhere (which itself requires PI-019). For new custom entities introduced in a single file, the single-owner pattern is fine; for native entities extended across multiple domains, it's awkward.

**Triggers seen.** 2026-05-19 CBM conversation on splitting Sponsor/Partner fields out of MN-Account.yaml. With current engine: MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml cannot contribute a Sponsor Profile panel to the Account layout because MN-Account.yaml would clobber it (or vice versa). Workaround: keep all Account-layout panels in MN-Account.yaml.

**Goal.** A YAML author can declare a `layout:` block in any YAML that targets an entity, and the engine merges all panels from all batched YAMLs targeting that entity into a single deployed layout. Panel ordering is deterministic (by file alphabetical order, then by declaration order within a file). Conflicts (e.g., two files declaring a panel with the same `label`) are flagged as deploy errors.

**Files to touch.**
1. `espo_impl/core/layout_manager.py:56` (`LayoutManager.process_layouts`) тАФ currently called once per YAML file with that file's `entity.layout` block. Refactor entry point so it accepts the full batch (or its layout contributions per entity) and emits one consolidated `save_layout` call per (entity, layoutType).
2. `espo_impl/workers/run_worker.py` тАФ change the orchestration so layout writes run AFTER all per-file field/relationship/entity work completes (currently layouts run per-file in the field/layout/relationship phase). New phase: "AggregateLayouts" between the per-file work and the workflows phase.
3. `espo_impl/core/layout_manager.py:392` (`_auto_generate_rows`) тАФ currently filters `field_definitions` by category against THIS file's fields. After aggregation, should consider all fields across all batched files targeting the entity. Depends on PI-019 (cross-file category resolution) being in place at validation time so the panels can validate.
4. New conflict-detection logic: two files contributing a panel with the same `label` for the same entity should produce a deploy error like `Layout conflict: Account.detail panel 'Client Profile' declared by MN-Account.yaml and CR-Account.yaml`.
5. Panel-order policy: document and implement deterministic ordering. Option A: file alphabetical order then declaration order. Option B: each file declares a `panelOrder` integer per panel. Option A is simpler; B is more flexible.

**Steps.**
1. Settle the panel-order policy (A or B). Add to the YAML schema doc.
2. Refactor `RunWorker` orchestration to defer layout writes until after the per-file work.
3. Add a `LayoutAggregator` (or extend `LayoutManager`) that collects layout contributions across the batch and emits one save per entity+layoutType.
4. Add the conflict-detection pass.
5. Update `_auto_generate_rows` to consult cross-file field definitions (depends on PI-019).
6. Update unit tests; add end-to-end tests that exercise the multi-file Account layout case.
7. Update `PRDs/product/app-yaml-schema.md` to document the aggregation semantics and panel-ordering rule.
8. Once landed, split CBM's MN-Account / MN-Partner-TEST / MN-Sponsor-TEST so each file contributes its own panel block and the engine merges them тАФ drop the SPLIT-NOTE caveats in those files.

**Validation.**
- `uv run pytest tests/ -k layout` passes (existing single-file tests must still pass).
- A new test deploys two YAMLs (MN-Account.yaml with Client Profile panel only, MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml with Sponsor Profile panel) and verifies the resulting EspoCRM Account detail layout has BOTH panels in deterministic order.
- A conflict test: two YAMLs declaring panels with the same label on the same entity produces a deploy error.

**References.**
- Conversation 2026-05-19 (CBM partner/sponsor YAML file-split exploration; the SPLIT-NOTE blocks in MN-Account.yaml / MN-Partner-TEST.yaml / MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml document this gap).
- Depends on PI-019 for the validator side (categories declared in sibling files must be resolvable at validation time).
- Related but distinct: the engine-bug-backlog entry about topological deploy ordering (relationship dependencies) тАФ separate concern, doesn't block this.
title: Cross-file layout aggregation in deploy engine

description: Two bugs surfaced during the 05-19-26 SES-001 paper-test apply attempt are addressed by a single workstream because they share a root cause (v0.5 slice D wired multi-tenancy at the DB-write path but missed two adjacent paths) and a single fix shape (extract resolve_active_engagement + route_settings_to_engagement helpers; centralized assert_export_dir_ready gate at all active export-write sites). Bug 1: API startup ignores current_engagement.json and connects to the post-migration empty v2.db, returning 'no such table: sessions' on every request тАФ cli.py:run_api never reads the marker file; UI-launched API works only because ui/app.py:_route_api_at_active_engagement inlines the env-var-set + cache-reset pattern before spawning the API subprocess. Bug 2: export hook writes snapshots against the engine-repo default Settings.export_dir regardless of active engagement; under any non-CRMBUILDER engagement, writes silently clobber CRMBUILDER's PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/. Same gap exists in force_export() and the catalog exporter. Scope per the slice plan at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/multi-tenancy-routing-fix-slice-plan.md: two slices (A backend, B UI). Slice A extracts the helpers, adds the CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR env var to Settings, adds the --engagement <code> CLI flag, wires cli.py:run_api to fail loud on no active engagement (per DEC-108), applies the assert_export_dir_ready gate at session_scope and force_export and the catalog exporter (per DEC-113), investigates and fixes bootstrap/migrate.py:64 per its scoping. Slice B refactors ui/app.py:_route_api_at_active_engagement and ui/active_engagement_context.py to use the shared helpers, surfaces the new failure conditions to operators via warning bands on Open Engagement (yellow for null export_dir, red for missing-on-disk), Edit Engagement dialog visual emphasis, about_dialog '(not configured)' / '(missing)' display, error-dialog handling for the new exceptions, and pytest-qt integration tests across a two-engagement state. The decisions captured at SES-044 are DEC-108 through DEC-114; they set out fresh-install behavior, missing-export_dir behavior, plumbing model, CLI flag inclusion, /admin/runtime-info deferral, centralized-gate scope, and fail-loud-on-missing-disk-path policy. Deferred from this workstream: cross-engagement references, snapshot replay/restore, engagement deletion, /admin/runtime-info diagnostic API endpoint (DEC-112; possibly opens as a follow-on PI if a real need arises). Discharge: slice B's commit lands; this PI moves to Closed (resolved) via the desktop UI's planning-items panel or a direct API write.
title: Complete v0.5 multi-tenancy routing: API startup engagement resolution + per-engagement export_dir

description:
DEC-117 through DEC-120 introduce six new entity types (workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event). After the schema-design workstream lands the entities and a build implements them in the database, the question is whether to retroactively populate records for pre-existing project state, or only go forward from the moment the entities ship.

Scope of the retroactive question:

(a) **Workstream records for completed workstreams.** Examples: the methodology entity schema-design workstream (SES-011..015 plus the SES-017 build-planning conversation plus the SES-019..024 v0.4 build conversations); the v0.5 engagement management workstream (SES-026..032 spanning multiple conversations); the v0.6 styling workstream (SES-036..043 spanning conversation 1, conversation 2, and seven build-execution conversations); the multi-tenancy routing fix workstream (SES-044..045 plus slice B); the catalog ingestion workstream (SES-016 or wherever it landed). Each has a clear identity, member conversations, and an outcome.

(b) **Conversation records for completed sessions.** All 45 existing sessions (SES-001 through SES-045) had a conversation behind them; each conversation has metadata (purpose, position-in-workstream where applicable, kickoff prompt path where one existed, predecessor and successor).

(c) **Reference book records for existing reference documents.** Workstream master plans, Product Requirements Documents (v0.1 through v0.6), implementation plans, methodology guides, schema specifications (the four methodology entity specs plus the soon-to-arrive six governance entity specs), the session-startup entry document (`crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md`), the apply script (`crmbuilder-v2/scripts/apply_close_out.py`).

(d) **Work ticket records for existing kickoff and execution prompts.** The four methodology schema-design kickoffs (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate), the methodology workstream-establishing planning prompt, the v0.4 through v0.6 planning prompts, all per-slice Claude Code execution prompts under `prompts/`.

(e) **Close-out payload records for existing payloads.** Every `close-out-payloads/ses_NNN.json` file (there are ~38 of them).

(f) **Deposit event records for past applies.** Reconstructable from commit history (git log of the relevant payload-apply commits), but the apply outcomes (success/partial/failure) are not in the commit log; outcome reconstruction would be assumptive (assume success unless commit message indicates otherwise).

Resolution paths to consider at the workstream-establishing conversation or at the per-entity schema-design conversations:

1. **Go-forward only.** No backfill. Record entities exist only for work that happens after the entities ship. Pre-existing project state has no governance entity records. Queries about pre-shipped state continue to work via the existing free-text mentions in session records.

2. **Selective backfill.** Backfill the entity types where reconstruction is high-fidelity (workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload) but skip deposit event because reconstructing outcomes is assumptive.

3. **Full backfill with reconstructed outcomes.** Backfill all six entity types including deposit events; outcome reconstruction tags assumptive entries explicitly.

Resolution is not blocking for the workstream itself тАФ the schema design proceeds with the entities defined for go-forward use; backfill is a follow-on decision at the build-planning conversation or later.

Discharge: closed when a decision is reached and (if backfill is chosen) the backfill is executed.
title: Retroactive migration: whether to backfill governance entity records for sessions, decisions, planning items, references, and prior workstreams already in the database
